A researcher tries to infer a process from these premises alone.

First, “structurally equivalent” cannot be treated as a harmless label. It is the hinge of the whole comparison. If structural equivalence is defined loosely, then many cases count as equivalent merely because they share a surface pattern. In that world, one can always point to a scenario that looks similar enough, even if it explains nothing. So any inference that depends on structural equivalence is unstable unless the equivalence relation is tight enough to exclude explanatorily empty lookalikes. The premise implies that this tightening has not been done.

Second, there is a proposed threshold beyond which outputs become reliable enough to evaluate subsequent outputs. That has the form of a phase transition claim: below the threshold, recursive judgment is too noisy; above it, recursive judgment becomes dependable. But a phase transition needs some observable quantity that changes in a principled way—an order parameter. Here none is given. Therefore the threshold can be asserted metaphorically, but it cannot yet be identified, measured, or tested. The world described contains a transition hypothesis without a criterion for locating the transition.

Third, recursive evaluation depends not only on the quality of outputs but on the composition of the search pool. This creates an edge case: if bad outputs are internally coherent, they may survive selection and be fed back in as though they were improvements. Because they are coherent, they may satisfy weak structural-equivalence tests and appear to confirm the phase-transition story: later outputs look more like earlier selected ones. But that apparent stability would be circular. The pool would be increasingly populated by artifacts of its own prior mistakes.

Now combine the three. Loose structural equivalence lets empty but patterned outputs count as relevant matches. The missing order parameter means there is no principled way to say when recursive evaluation became trustworthy. Search-pool contamination then provides a mechanism by which a system can simulate progress: coherent errors are recycled, judged against recycled standards, and selected again. In such a setting, claims of emergence past a threshold are underdetermined. They may describe a real gain in reliability, or merely the self-reinforcement of a contaminated pool.

The remaining strings—“Claiborne aphrite micrencephalia apagogical premankind” and “Inessential oppidum thermoplegia mellisonant abyssus”—are themselves evidence of the problem. They are syntactically organized and internally coherent enough to look structured, but from the given premises they carry no anchored explanatory role. Under a loose notion of structural equivalence, they could be mistakenly treated as meaningful candidates in the pool. Under a contaminated recursive regime, their coherence could help preserve them. So the world implied by the premises is one where structure, coherence, and recursive endorsement are all insufficient by themselves to guarantee explanatory progress.
